channel.title: Tech Insights
channel.description: Daily curated news on AI, developer tools, and the tech industry

item.title: My 2026 Productivity Stack
item.description: 2026 Producitvity Stack Hey 👋 Every year or so I like to write about the tools I'm using. Partly because I enjoy reading other people's setups, partly because it forces me to think about whether...

item.title: You’re Not Building Apps. You’re Designing Systems.
item.description: Most developers think they’re building applications. Pages. APIs. Features. Endpoints. Something users can click, interact with, and use. But after building enough projects, something becomes...

item.title: I wanted to understand Payments better ... and learned about HAR files.
item.description: I started following up on my challenge of learning more about how payments in Web2 work: I set up my account, my sandbox, and a simple checkout flow. I went through the flow once, and it worked....

item.title: Live updates from Elon Musk and Sam Altman’s court battle over the future of OpenAI
item.description: Sam Altman and Elon Musk are facing off in a high-stakes trial that could alter the future of OpenAI and its most well-known product, ChatGPT. In 2024, Musk filed a lawsuit accusing OpenAI of...

item.title: Top 7 Featured DEV Posts of the Week
item.description: Welcome to this week's Top 7, where the DEV editorial team handpicks their favorite posts from the previous week (Saturday-Friday). Congrats to all the authors that made it onto the list 👏 I...

item.title: Indexing every WHERE column is not PostgreSQL optimization
item.description: One PostgreSQL indexing mistake I see often: “The query filters on A, B and C, so let’s create an index on A, B, C.” That may work, but it may also be the wrong index. For composite B-tree...

item.title: 🔥 PostgreSQL vs SQLite – which one should you actually use for your web app or SaaS?
item.description: The choice between SQLite and PostgreSQL is a frequent point of confusion. The quick choice is: For almost any public-facing web app or SaaS, you want PostgreSQL. SQLite is for embedded,...

item.title: Model Showdown Round 4: Opus vs Qwen — Writers, Not Coders
item.description: Two models. Same prompt. Same five fodder files. Same 27 published posts to check for redundancy. Same writing style guide. One chose the Dev.to syndication saga. The other chose the tag taxonomy...

item.title: GM settles California lawsuit claiming it sold driving habit data to insurance companies
item.description: General Motors has agreed to pay $12.75 million to settle a California data privacy lawsuit that accused the automaker of selling driver location and driver data, as reported earlier by Reuters....

item.title: Cloud Barata e Potente: Meu Guia de Bolso para Devs e Startups
item.description: Gurizada, vou ser direto: cansei de ver projeto bom morrer na praia porque o custo da infraestrutura virou um monstro de sete cabeças. Como CTO, lido dia a dia com GCP e Oracle Cloud para a...

item.title: GarlicStamp: an open identity protocol for AI agents
item.description: If you've spent any time wiring AI agents into real systems — picking up tickets, executing trades, managing infrastructure, talking to APIs that cost real money — you've already run into the...

item.title: LSM Trees: Why Your Database Writes Are Fast and Your Reads Are Lying to You
item.description: TL;DR: The thing that broke my comfortable ignorance about storage engines was a pipeline ingesting sensor telemetry — about 50,000 inserts per second into a PostgreSQL 15 cluster. The hardware...

item.title: Matter and OpenADR team up to connect smart homes to the grid
item.description: Smart energy management just took a step closer to becoming simpler. This week, the organizations behind Matter, the smart-home interoperability standard, and the OpenADR protocol, which sends...

item.title: Joanna Stern is not a robot, but she lived with them
item.description: My guest today is longtime friend of the show Joanna Stern. You all know Joanna: she is the former senior personal technology columnist for The Wall Street Journal, a former Decoder guest host,...

item.title: Fostering breakthrough AI innovation through customer-back engineering
item.description: Despite years of digitization, organizations capture less than one-third of the value expected from digital investments, according to McKinsey research. That’s because most big companies begin...

item.title: TikTok is letting UK users pay to remove ads
item.description: TikTok is preparing to roll out a paid, ad-free version of its app in the UK. The new subscription announced today will be available to users over 18 "over the coming months," according to...

item.title: There aren’t enough rockets for space data centers. Cowboy Space raised $275 million to build them.
item.description: Cowboy Space Corporation wants to put data centers in orbit. First, it has to build the rockets to get them there.

item.title: Innovation abounds in device charging
item.description: The changes may be less perceptible than in smartphones, tablets, or wearables, but chargers have also been quietly reinvented over the last decade. At one time a bulky mix of tangled cables and...

item.title: Implementing advanced AI technologies in finance
item.description: In finance departments that have long been defined by precision and control, AI has arrived less as a neatly managed upgrade than as a quiet insurgency. Employees are already using it while...

item.title: Discord adds a free Xbox Game Pass ‘starter edition’ for Nitro subscribers
item.description: Discord is announcing the launch of Nitro Rewards today, a new program for subscribers that bundles benefits from third parties like Microsoft, Logitech, and SteelSeries, into a Nitro...

item.title: Wordle is turning into a primetime TV game show on NBC
item.description: Wordle could be the next great game show. NBC has greenlit a game show version of The New York Times' popular puzzle game, and it's set to debut in 2027 with Savannah Guthrie as the host. The show...

item.title: ⚡ Weekly Recap: Linux Rootkit, macOS Crypto Stealer, WebSocket Skimmers and More
item.description: Rough Monday. Somebody poisoned a trusted download again, somebody else turned cloud servers into public housing, and a few crews are still getting into boxes with bugs that should’ve died years...

item.title: The Download: the hantavirus outbreak and Musk v. Altman week 2
item.description: This is today’s edition of The Download, our weekday newsletter that provides a daily dose of what’s going on in the world of technology. Here’s what you need to know about the cruise ship...

item.title: Venmo finally takes privacy seriously
item.description: Venmo is starting to test a big redesign of its app, and as part of the changes, it will be implementing a major new privacy measure: the onboarding process for new users will set their posts to...

item.title: Your Purple Team Isn't Purple — It's Just Red and Blue in the Same Room
item.description: Defending a network at 2 am looks a lot like this: an analyst copy-pasting a hash from a PDF into a SIEM query. A red team script is being rewritten by hand so the blue team can use it. A patch...

item.title: Windows 11 is getting a macOS-like speed boost
item.description: Microsoft is currently testing a new speed boost feature in Windows 11 that is designed to improve app launch times and make things like the Start menu feel more responsive. The feature, which is...

item.title: LLMs and Text-in-Text Steganography
item.description: Turns out that LLMs are really good at hiding text messages in other text messages.

item.title: Forza Horizon 6 has been leaked and cracked a week before its release
item.description: The full version of Playground Games' upcoming Forza Horizon 6 game has leaked online over the weekend and has already been cracked by pirates. The download appeared on file sharing sites after...

item.title: CUDA Proves Nvidia Is a Software Company
item.description: There’s a deep, forbidding moat that surrounds Nvidia—and it has nothing to do with hardware.

item.title: I Work in Hollywood. Everyone Who Used to Make TV Is Now Secretly Training AI
item.description: For screenwriters like me—and job seekers all over—AI gig work is the new waiting tables. In eight months, I’ve done 20 of these soul-crushing contracts for five different platforms. It’s bad.
